Content_Sketch: The outer borough house: comfortable, brick, but not Manhattan — the gap is visible from the window
Illustration_Note: Child hands at window frame — Manhattan skyline impossibly bright across the water in the distance

Content_Sketch: Father's lesson delivered daily: the name on a building is the only thing that cannot be taken from you
Illustration_Note: FATHER stickman gesturing at building model on desk — child stickman watching from corner of room

Content_Sketch: At age 8, you understand what the skyline means — not as scenery, but as a destination
Illustration_Note: Close-up POV: child hands pressed against car window, Manhattan towers filling the glass

Content_Sketch: The name is not inherited — it is built. And you have already decided you will build it bigger.
Illustration_Note: Dark background. Bold white text: 'THE NAME IS NOT INHERITED. IT IS BUILT.'

Content_Sketch: You are six. The outer borough house is large by any measure — except the one you've already chosen.
Illustration_Note: Small stickman standing in front of large brick house — Manhattan silhouette on far horizon, visually larger than the house

Content_Sketch: Your father leaves before sunrise and returns with new deals — you sit at the top of the stairs and listen to the numbers
Illustration_Note: Small stickman seated at staircase top in shadow — FATHER below at desk, papers spread, animated in discussion

Content_Sketch: At 13, your father sends you to military academy — not as punishment, as instruction in winning
Illustration_Note: Two-panel: child at home, casual — then same figure in military jacket uniform at stone academy gate

Content_Sketch: The academy teaches: every room is a competition — figure out the hierarchy on day one and place yourself above it
Illustration_Note: Teen stickman in military jacket standing in front of formation of identical uniformed stickmen — slightly taller, slightly ahead

Content_Sketch: You graduate the academy knowing how to read a room — a skill your father never taught directly but always practiced
Illustration_Note: Teen stickman walking out of academy gate, FATHER outside waiting — neither reaches out, just starts walking

Content_Sketch: Business school: you do not belong to this world yet — you take notes on who does and how they got there
Illustration_Note: Young stickman at lecture hall, surrounded by peer stickmen — observing rather than participating, slightly to the side

Content_Sketch: You graduate 1968. Your father hands you the company keys — and with them, everything he never finished.
Illustration_Note: FATHER stickman extending document shape toward young adult stickman — both standing in outer borough property office, blueprints visible on walls

Content_Sketch: 25 years old. You leave the outer borough and walk into Manhattan — not as a visitor. As the next owner.
Illustration_Note: Two-panel: young stickman at outer borough building — then same stickman in Manhattan street, taller buildings surroundin him

Content_Sketch: First Manhattan deal: a crumbling hotel on 42nd Street that everyone said was unfixable — you fix it and put your name on it
Illustration_Note: Stickman with AIDES_BUSINESS pointing at building model — old run-down building shape in background transforms across composition

Content_Sketch: The first building with your name on it: a revelation. The name draws people in before you say a word.
Illustration_Note: Stickman standing at building entrance — AIDES_BUSINESS flanking, suited stickmen approaching from street toward entrance

Content_Sketch: 1983: 68 floors on Fifth Avenue. Gold letters, six feet tall, at the top. The city cannot look away.
Illustration_Note: Wide shot: stickman standing at base of 68-floor tower — looking up at gold nameplate shape on glass facade, city behind smaller

Content_Sketch: First wife: she builds alongside you — the company files with two names on them, the tower decorated in both directions
Illustration_Note: SUBJECT and FIRST_WIFE stickman side by side at drafting table with building blueprints — AIDES_BUSINESS in background

Content_Sketch: Casino expansion: Atlantic City means more deals, more debt, more of your name on more buildings than your cash flow can support
Illustration_Note: Stickman standing in neon-lit casino interior — large space, many floors visible, but stacks of document shapes accumulating at sides

Content_Sketch: The tabloids give you a name — a nickname that belongs to the brand now, not just to you
Illustration_Note: Stickman at press conference podium, camera flashes as silhouettes in front, microphone cluster shape at podium center

Content_Sketch: You notice: the debt is growing faster than the buildings. But the name keeps growing faster than the debt.
Illustration_Note: Stickman alone at desk late at night — documents and financial pages spread, city lights through window behind, posture forward and tense

Content_Sketch: 1990: markets collapse. Personal debt reaches $900 million. Bankers request a meeting no one wants to have.
Illustration_Note: Stickman at boardroom table surrounded by AIDES_BUSINESS in panic postures — documents and phones spread, atmosphere of emergency

Content_Sketch: First wife and first marriage — both unwind in the same season as the finances. Two structures down at once.
Illustration_Note: Stickman in empty luxury apartment interior — FIRST_WIFE figure walking away frame left, stickman still at center, standing

Content_Sketch: Bankers propose liquidation. You propose an alternative: the name is worth more intact than erased.
Illustration_Note: Stickman at one end of long boardroom table — multiple shorter banker stickmen on other side with document stacks, neither side moving

Content_Sketch: Your second wife: she appears during the wreckage. Her warmth is not a contradiction — it is a refusal to treat this as the end.
Illustration_Note: Stickman in Florida estate setting — SECOND_WIFE figure beside, warmer posture than FIRST_WIFE, background is pool and palm trees

Content_Sketch: Six corporate bankruptcies — filed, restructured, absorbed. The name survives each one, put on something new before the last one falls.
Illustration_Note: Stickman walking away from crumbling building silhouette on left — placing name shape on new building facade on right

Content_Sketch: You choose: stay visible. Stay loud. Do not disappear. The name is still there — as long as you keep using it.
Illustration_Note: Stickman facing outward, posture straight after pressure — city behind, no agents yet, alone but standing

Content_Sketch: 2004. A television studio calls. They want you to be yourself — on camera, for millions of people.
Illustration_Note: Two-panel: stickman at boardroom — then same stickman in television studio spotlit boardroom set, cameras visible as shapes

Content_Sketch: 'You're fired.' Two words, weekly, prime time. The name enters homes that have never seen a building with it on the facade.
Illustration_Note: Stickman at studio boardroom head, pointing toward door — row of shorter contestant stickmen looking toward them, cameras around

Content_Sketch: 14 seasons. Brand extensions: beef, water, vodka, casinos rebuilt, university courses, golf courses. The name on everything.
Illustration_Note: Stickman in center — displayed around them: small product silhouette shapes (bottle, tower, golf flag, diploma shape) in orbit

Content_Sketch: Third wife — she comes from a small country in Eastern Europe. Composed, unhurried, never competing for the room.
Illustration_Note: Stickman and THIRD_WIFE at formal event — THIRD_WIFE in elegant posture beside, not behind, both facing forward

Content_Sketch: You watch the presidential debates. You think: these men are not better than you at this. They just started earlier.
Illustration_Note: Stickman on couch watching TV — TV shows debating politician silhouettes, stickman leaning forward, evaluating

Content_Sketch: You have put your name on everything you could reach. One thing remains that has never had your name on it.
Illustration_Note: Dark background with single building silhouette fading in. Text: 'EVERYTHING. EXCEPT THE ONE THING.'

Content_Sketch: 2015: you announce the campaign from the lobby of your own tower — the one with your name on it in gold.
Illustration_Note: Stickman at podium in tower lobby — grand marble and gold interior behind, packed with crowd and media cameras

Content_Sketch: The political establishment does not treat you as a threat — they treat you as a spectacle. That is their error.
Illustration_Note: Left: stickman at podium gesturing to crowd. Right: establishment stickmen at table laughing, smaller frames

Content_Sketch: A recording surfaces — the kind that ends campaigns. Your team prepares for shutdown. You do not.
Illustration_Note: Stickman standing with AIDES_CAMPAIGN clustered around, arms in distress gestures — stickman posture unchanged, forward

Content_Sketch: Election night, November 2016. The map fills in. No one broadcast it predicted this — including, privately, some of your own team.
Illustration_Note: POV hand at desk, US map shape on screen filling with result shapes — room in background of shocked AIDES_CAMPAIGN stickmen gesturing

Content_Sketch: You move into the house on Pennsylvania Avenue. 132 rooms. The biggest name you have ever put on anything.
Illustration_Note: Stickman walking into White House formal entrance flanked by AGENTS — building facade enormous in background, formal and historic

Content_Sketch: Every week is a headline. Every decision is a war. The name draws both devotion and fury in equal, unprecedented quantities.
Illustration_Note: Stickman at Oval Office desk with document shapes — outside window: CROWD mass with arms raised, inside: AIDES_CAMPAIGN clustered

Content_Sketch: First impeachment proceedings: what they try, and do not complete. You remain. The name remains.
Illustration_Note: Stickman in formal chamber setting — surrounded by formal stickmen in congressional arrangement, posture unchanged, forward-directed

Content_Sketch: Second impeachment. Leave office without conceding — the name does not concede. It restructures.
Illustration_Note: Stickman exiting White House via helicopter shape — AGENTS flanking, posture upright, no backward glance — Florida estate implied in next frame

Content_Sketch: Criminal indictment. Then conviction. 34 counts. You announce your candidacy the same week.
Illustration_Note: Stickman in courtroom setting — formal interior, flanked by AGENTS, surrounded by formal legal stickmen, stickman posture controlled and still

Content_Sketch: 2024. You win. The same name. The same playbook. The same person.
Illustration_Note: POV hands at a podium — CROWD mass in background filling frame with raised arms, CHILDREN flanking at sides of podium

Content_Sketch: Return: the tower on Fifth Avenue. The gold letters. The name, six feet tall. Still there.
Illustration_Note: Wide shot: SUBJECT stickman standing at base of tower — looking up at gold nameplate shape, same composition as BEAT_03_04 but stickman now full PHASE_PRESENT traits — older, heavier, same hair, same tie

Content_Sketch: The tower will outlast this version of you. The name will remain on the stone. What it will mean — that question has never been answered.
Illustration_Note: Stickman centered in frame, background fades to white on one side, tower facade visible on other — posture still but the weight of future is in the dot eyes directed upward

Content_Sketch: You have put your name on everything. One thing you cannot put it on: what happens after.
Illustration_Note: Black background. A single line of white text fading in slowly: 'THE NAME STAYS. THE QUESTION DOES NOT.'
